slides.notes: Welcome to Macroeconomics 101. The masthead rules set the tone — a serious financial paper. Everything auto-reveals — no clicks. Emphasize the $105T world-GDP stat: larger than every public equity market combined. Six slides ahead: three indicators, one identity, one historical episode (CPI), the Phillips curve, and the policy toolkit.

elements.alt: Neoclassical stone facade of a central bank building at golden hour — columns and carved pediment in warm amber light, used as the right-side hero illustration

elements.content: Fig. 1 · Central bank facade · golden hour
A six-slide primer on the three numbers that define the business cycle — and the tools central banks use to lean against it.

nominal, World Bank
larger than every public equity market combined
INSIDE:  01 Three key indicators   ·   02 GDP identity   ·   03 Inflation & the CPI   ·   04 Phillips curve   ·   05 Policy toolkit

slides.notes:
Three indicators. On enter: section tag, rule, title "What economists watch", intro subtitle.

[step:1]Indicator 01 · GDP reveals — number + heading + body + stat line "US 2024: $28.1T, +2.8% YoY".[/step]

[step:2]Indicator 02 · Inflation reveals — US CPI 2024 +3.0%, Fed target 2.0%.[/step]

[step:3]Indicator 03 · Unemployment reveals — US U-3 4.1%, NAIRU ~4.4%.[/step]

[step:4]Bottom rule + pullquote: "When two of the three move opposite expectations, the policy argument gets loud."[/step]

Rule of thumb: two usually move together, one lags. Policy arguments live in divergence.

elements.content: Three numbers, three windows onto the same economy. Two move together, one lags — and the interesting story is always in how they diverge.
Total market value of final goods and services produced in a period. The headline number everyone quotes — and everyone argues about.
Rate at which the price level rises. Measured by CPI or the GDP deflator. Moderate is healthy; runaway is corrosive to savings and contracts.
Share of the labor force actively seeking work. Tracks the business cycle and sets the political temperature of every news cycle.
*"When two of the three move opposite expectations, the policy argument gets loud."*

slides.notes:
GDP expenditure identity. On enter: title + formula box "Y = C + I + G + (X − M)" auto-reveal.

[step:1]Bar chart cascades in — Consumption 68% → Investment 18% → Government 18% → Net exports −4% (negative bar in orange).[/step]

[step:2]Right-side legend table fades in — Term / Meaning / US share rows mirroring the bars.[/step]

In America, the consumer IS the economy — consumption dominates at ~68%.

CPI over years. On enter: section tag, title, the PGFPlots chart (left: CPI 2018–2024 with 2% Fed-target dashed line, 8% 2022 peak annotated).

[step:1]Right side "WHAT IT MEASURES" panel — CPI formula π_t = (P_t − P_{t-1})/P_{t-1}, basket composition (~34% shelter, ~13% food, ~7% energy), core vs headline distinction.[/step]

[step:2]Bottom "SHOCKS VS TRENDS" note — 2021–22 blended pandemic supply shocks with loose monetary policy; disentangling the two is an active research question.[/step]

Chart speaks first, commentary supports.

elements.content:
$$ \pi_t = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}} $$

The CPI is a weighted basket: ~34% shelter, ~13% food, ~7% energy, rest services and durable goods.

**Core CPI** strips volatile food & energy to show the persistent trend the Fed reacts to.
The 2021-22 spike blended pandemic-era supply shocks with loose monetary policy — disentangling the two is the active research question.

slides.notes:
The Phillips curve — sample points on a smooth curve. Auto-reveals from a single pgfplots figure so dots and curve share one coordinate space (the earlier hand-placed version had the markers drifting off the line).

[step:1]Sidebar "INTUITION" opens — read aloud: lower unemployment pushes wages and prices up in the short run.[/step]

[step:2]Bottom rule + line appears — the long-run story: Friedman/Phelps showed the curve shifts once expectations adjust.[/step]

elements.content:
In the short run, lower unemployment pushes wages — and therefore prices — up. Central banks exploit this to steer demand: tightening when inflation runs hot, easing when the cycle cools.

The long-run curve is vertical at the NAIRU: you can trade inflation for unemployment only temporarily.
The curve has shifted, flattened, and briefly disappeared across different decades — it is **empirical**, not mechanical.

slides.notes:
Monetary vs fiscal policy. On enter: title (auto).

[step:1]MONETARY chain (orange): rate cut → cheaper credit → investment & consumption ↑.[/step]

[step:2]FISCAL chain (blue): tax cut / spend up → household income ↑ → aggregate demand ↑.[/step]

[step:3]Both chains converge into the "GDP ↑" dark ellipse at the bottom — convergence lines draw in.[/step]

[step:4]Right-side "LAGS MATTER" panel — monetary 6–18 mo bite, reversible; fiscal slow to pass fast to land, politically sticky.[/step]

End of lecture — next term teases international trade + open-economy macro.

elements.content:
**Monetary:**  6–18 months to bite.  Decisions every 6 weeks. Reversible.

**Fiscal:**  slower to pass, faster to land. Politically sticky — easy to turn on, hard to unwind.
**End of lecture**   ·   Next term: international trade, exchange rates, and open-economy macro.
